standard_description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, how much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share 1/2 lb of chocolate equally? How many 1/3-cup servings are in 2 cups of raisins?
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{1\over3} \div 2}$$
b.
$${{1\over4} \div 9}$$
Problem 2
Zach spends half of his workday tutoring students. If he sees 5 students and spends the same amount of time with each student, what fraction of his workday does he spend tutoring each student?

Task
###TABLE0###
How many cups of salad dressing will this recipe make? Write an equation to represent your thinking. Assume that the herbs and salt do not change the amount of dressing.
If this recipe makes 6 servings, how much dressing would there be in one serving? Write a number sentence to represent your thinking.

Task

Problem 1
Nolan gives some Fruit-by-the-Foot to his
$$3$$
friends to share equally.
a.   If he has
$$3$$
feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
b.   If he has
$$1$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
c.   If he has
$${1\over2}$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
d.   If he has
$${1\over3}$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
Problem 2
Priya and Mai used the diagrams below to find the value of
$$\frac13 \div 4$$
.
###TABLE0###
a.   What is the same about the diagrams?
b.   What is different?
c.   Find the value that makes the equation true.
$$\frac13 \div 4 =$$
____
Problem 3
a.    Solve. Then check your work.
$${{1\over5}\div2}$$
$${{1\over6}\div4}$$
$${{1\over9}\div6}$$
b.   What do you notice in Part (a)? What do you wonder?

Task
Julius has 4 blue marbles.  If one third of Julius' marbles are blue,
how many marbles does Julius have?  Draw a diagram and explain.

Problem 1
Jenny buys 2 feet of string. If this is one-third the amount she needs to make a bracelet, how many feet will she need?
a.   Draw a diagram to represent the problem.
b.   Write an expression to represent the problem.
c.   Find how many feet of string Jenny needs.
Problem 2
a.   Match each division expression (cut out from
Template: Fraction Division Sort
) with a situation (cut out from
Template: Fraction Division Sort
). Some expressions do not have a matching situation.
b.   Answer each question. Be prepared to share your reasoning.
Problem 3
a.   Write a story context that can be solved using the expression
$${2\div{1\over4}}$$
.
b.   Write a story context that can be solved using the expression
$${{1\over3}\div4}$$
.

Problem 1
Anej has
$$1\over2$$
of his birthday cake leftover. He wants to share it equally with his 3 friends. What fraction of the original cake should Anej and his 3 friends get?
Problem 2
Norma has 8 red colored pencils. This is
$$1\over3$$
of the total colored pencils she has. How many colored pencils does Norma have altogether?

Task
Alysha really wants to ride her favorite ride at the amusement park one more time before her parents pick her up at 2:30 pm. There is a very long line at this ride, which Alysha joins at 1:50 pm (point A in the diagram below). Alysha is nervously checking the time as she is moving forward in the line. By 2:03 she has made it to point B in line.
What is your best estimate for how long it will take Alysha to reach the front of the line? If the ride lasts 3 minutes, can she ride one more time before her parents arrive?

Problem 1
Jenny buys
$$2$$
feet of ribbon to make bracelets. Different ways to make bracelets require different amounts of string, depending on how complicated the designs are.
a.   If Jenny uses
$$2$$
feet of string to make a bracelet, how many bracelets can she make?
b.   If she uses
$$1$$
foot of string to make a bracelet, how many bracelets can she make?
c.   If she uses
$$\frac{1}{2}$$
foot of string to make a bracelet, how many bracelets can she make?
d.   If she uses
$$\frac{1}{3}$$
foot of string to make a bracelet, how many bracelets can she make?
e.   If she uses
$$\frac{1}{4}$$
foot of string to make a bracelet, how many bracelets can she make?
Problem 2
a.    Solve. Then check your work.
$${3\div{1\over4}}$$
$${5\div{1\over3}}$$
$$8\div \frac{1}{7}$$
b.   What do you notice in Part (a)? What do you wonder?

a.
$${6\div {1\over2}}$$
b.
$${3\div {1\over5}}$$

Mr. Shirazi has 2 feet of fabric. He wants to make bookmarks that each uses a sixth of a foot of fabric as Eid al-Fitr gifts. Does he have enough fabric to make 10 gifts?

topic_path: Adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators

hints:
Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{2}$ | $2, {4}, \underline{6}$
$\purpleD{3}$ | $3, \underline{6}, 9$

The least common denominator is ${6}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $6$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{6}{2}}=\dfrac{\greenD{6} \times 3}{\greenD{2} \times 3} = {\dfrac\greenD{18}\greenD{6}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{1} \times 2}{\purpleD{3} \times 2} = {\dfrac\purpleD{2}\purpleD{6}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{6}{2}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}} = \dfrac{16}{6}$

We can also write $\dfrac{16}{6}$ as $\dfrac83$ or $2\dfrac23$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation is true when the left and right sides of the equal sign have the same value.

Let's compare the values of each side of the equations.

##Is $\dfrac8{10} - \dfrac2{10} = \dfrac{10}{10}$ true?


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac8{10}} - \greenD{\dfrac2{10}}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac6{10}}$, so the equation is *not* true.

##Is $\dfrac4{12} - \dfrac3{12} = \dfrac2{12}$ true?



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac4{12}} - \greenD{\dfrac3{12}}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac1{12}}$, so the equation is *not* true.

##Is $\dfrac42 - \dfrac12 = \dfrac32$ true?



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac42} - \greenD{\dfrac12}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$, so the equation *is* true.

khan_exercises.title: Add and subtract mixed numbers (with regrouping)
khan_exercises.description: Practice adding and subtracting mixed numbers with common (like) denominators. Items require regrouping (borrowing).
topic_path: Get ready for adding and subtracting fractions
Add and subtract mixed numbers (with regrouping)

hints:
Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} + \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{5}} =  \blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}} + \blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$ \maroonD{\dfrac54}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{12}} + \blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{12}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{17}{12}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}} + \blueD2 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{6}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}} + \blueD2 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{11}{8}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}} + \blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than $1$. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{13}{10}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} - \blueD2 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{2}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD4\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} =\blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{4}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}} - \blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}&=\blueD7+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
&=\blueD7+ \maroonD{\dfrac44}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
&=\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{4}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD5\maroonD{ \dfrac{2}{5}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}&=\blueD4+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
&=\blueD4+ \maroonD{\dfrac55}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
&=\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}} - \blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
-&\blueD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}&=\blueD6+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
&=\blueD6+ \maroonD{\dfrac66}+\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
&=\blueD6 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{6}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$
First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$.
We need to regroup $\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}&=\blueD4+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD4+ \maroonD{\dfrac88}+\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{12}{8}}
\end{align}$
We can replace $\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ with $\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{12}{8}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$
Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$
Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
&\,\ \blueD1&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$
${1 \dfrac{5}{8}}={5 \dfrac{4}{8}} - {3\dfrac{7}{8}} $

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{6} \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}} - \blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD{6}\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}&=\blueD5+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
&=\blueD5+ \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{10}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
&=\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{16}{10}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}\\
\hline\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}&=\blueD7+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7+ \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{8}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{8}}
\end{align}$
